coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled mesoporous silica decorated with biogenic carbon dot nanospheres hybrid nanomaterial for efficient removal of aqueous Methoxy-DDT via a Short-Bed Adsorption column technique
coredata.dc:creator: Parambil, Ajith Manayil
Priyadarshini, Eepsita
Goutam, Rohit
Tsai, Pei-Chien
Huang, Po-Chin
Rajamani, Paulraj
Lin, Yuan-Chung
Ponnusamy, Vinoth Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Methoxy-DDT is an organochlorine pesticide extensively used in agricultural practices as a DDT substitute. Methoxy-DDT has been found and quantified in several investigations in groundwater, drinking water, sediment, and various biota. Therefore, designing efficient and cost-effective adsorbents for removing methoxy-DDT is vital. In this work, we embedded Ficus benghalensis L. derived carbon dots (CDs) in mesoporous silica (MS) to fabricate MS-CDs nanohybrid material. MS-CDs nanohybrid exhibited remarkable selectivity and removal efficiency towards methoxy-DDT, outperforming other endocrine disruptors. Parameters for industrial-scale fixed-bed adsorption columns, such as bed capacity, length, and breakthrough times, were analyzed. The kinetic study revealed that pseudo-second-order (PSO) adsorption and isotherm analysis confirmed the Langmuir model as the best fit. Small bed adsorption (SBA) column analysis was carried out using spiked Yamuna river water, and the breakthrough curves were demonstrated by varying MS-CDs bed height. The maximum adsorption capacity obtained for methoxy-DDT was 17.16 mg/g at breakthrough and 49.98 mg/g at exhaustion. The adsorbent showed 86.53% removal efficiency in the 5th cycle, demonstrating good reusability. These results indicate that the developed material MS-CDs-based organic sphere is an effective adsorbent for aqueous methoxy-DDT adsorption and can be applied to wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Mesoporous silica (MS)
Endocrine disruptors
Methoxy-DDT
Adsorption-kinetics
Environmental water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CDs and MS-CDs nanohybrid
Characterization of synthesized CDs and MS-CDs
Column purification of Yamuna River water using MS-CDs adsorbent
Desorption and reuse of spent MS-CDs
Fabrication and characterization of CDs modified mesoporous silica (MS-CDs)
Effect of initial pH on methoxy-DDT adsorption
Column purification of Yamuna River water using MS-CDs adsorbent
Desorption and reuse of spent MS-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: INSPIRE Senior Research Fellowship (Grant No. IF 180104) to AMP from the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Gov. of India, is acknowledged. This work was partially supported by the Research Center for Precision Environmental Medicine, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan, from the Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan and by the Kaohsiung Medical University Research Center Grant (KMU-TC113A01). This research work was also supported financially by the grants NSTC110-2113-M-037-009-, and NSTC 112-2113-M-037-005- from the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan. The authors are thankful to operators and gratefully acknowledge the use of HR-TEM, FE-SEM, pXRD, and XPS equipment provided by the Instrument Center (core facility) of National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan, and instrument facilities provided by National Sun-yet Sen University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan, for this research work. In addition, we also thank KMU-NHRI for their bilateral collaborative research grant support (NHRIKMU-113-I005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Carbon dots. (a) UV–visible absorption spectra. (b) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CDs. Inset. Blue-green fluorescence of CDs under UV-irradiation (c & d) HRTEM image of CDs in magnifications 50 nm and 10 nm, respectively. d Inset. SEAD pattern of CDs. (e) Dynamic Light scattering (DLS) spectra showing the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra show decreased adsorption intensity of unreacted CDs in suspension after interaction with mesoporous silica (MS). Inset. Colour of MS and MS-CDs, (b) FESEM image of MS, (c and d) FESEM image of CDs assembled MS.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, MS, & MS-CDs. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of MS & MS-CDs. (c) Pore size distribution curves of MS & MS-CDs.
XPS spectra of CDs on MS. (a) Full survey spectra. (b–f) Si2p, Si2s, C1s, N1s and O1s functional groups.
(a) Effect of initial pH on the adsorption capacities of Methoxy-DDT on MS and MS-CDs. (b) Methoxy-DDT phenolic derivative formation in acidic medium.

Adsorption kinetics and isotherm of methoxy-DDT adsorption on the MS-CDs. (a) Pseudo-second-order model (PSO) (b) Pseudo-first-order model (PFO) (c) Intra-particle diffusion model (IPD) (d) Langmuir Isotherm (e) Freundlich Isotherm (f) Temkin Isotherm.

(a) Schematics of Short Bed Adsorption (SBA) Column packed with MS-CDs (b) Expanded breakthrough curve of 10g MS-CDs SBA column. (c) Five cycles of Methoxy-DDT removal by MS-CDs SBA column, indicating the potential of reusability of MS-CDs.
The list of surface functional groups obtained from FTIR spectra of CDs, MS, and MS-CDs.
PFO, PSO, and IPD constants (K1, K2, Kdiff) for adsorption of methoxy-DDT into MS-CDs and Isotherm parameters calculated from the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms.
Computed column experimental parameters at flow rate 0.03L/min and initial concentration of 100 mg/L.

row.entry: Amount adsorbed at full bed saturation

head.ce:title: Self-assembled mesoporous silica decorated with biogenic carbon dot nanospheres hybrid nanomaterial for efficient removal of aqueous Methoxy-DDT via a Short-Bed Adsorption column technique

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Engineering & Center for Emerging Contaminants Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methoxy-DDT is an organochlorine pesticide extensively used in agricultural practices as a DDT substitute. Methoxy-DDT has been found and quantified in several investigations in groundwater, drinking water, sediment, and various biota. Therefore, designing efficient and cost-effective adsorbents for removing methoxy-DDT is vital. In this work, we embedded Ficus benghalensis L. derived carbon dots (CDs) in mesoporous silica (MS) to fabricate MS-CDs nanohybrid material. MS-CDs nanohybrid exhibited remarkable selectivity and removal efficiency towards methoxy-DDT, outperforming other endocrine disruptors. Parameters for industrial-scale fixed-bed adsorption columns, such as bed capacity, length, and breakthrough times, were analyzed. The kinetic study revealed that pseudo-second-order (PSO) adsorption and isotherm analysis confirmed the Langmuir model as the best fit. Small bed adsorption (SBA) column analysis was carried out using spiked Yamuna river water, and the breakthrough curves were demonstrated by varying MS-CDs bed height. The maximum adsorption capacity obtained for methoxy-DDT was 17.16 mg/g at breakthrough and 49.98 mg/g at exhaustion. The adsorbent showed 86.53% removal efficiency in the 5th cycle, demonstrating good reusability. These results indicate that the developed material MS-CDs-based organic sphere is an effective adsorbent for aqueous methoxy-DDT adsorption and can be applied to wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are chemicals used to protect plants against pests, illnesses, and weeds and enhance the shelf-life of food items. Methoxy-DDT, an organochlorine pesticide (OCP), has been used as a DDT substitute (Rajmohan et al., 2020). For more than 15 years, it has been banned or restricted in many nations. There is no publicly accessible information on the quantity of methoxy-DDT currently produced or used. Methoxy-DDT has been found and quantified in several investigations in sediment, water, groundwater, drinking water, and various biota (Rajmohan et al., 2020). The environment does not naturally contain methoxy-DDT. Based on the data issued in 1990 by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), agricultural soil pollutants are responsible for 50% of the water contamination in rivers and lakes. Pesticides are intentionally dumped into aquatic habitats by soil erosion, aerial spraying, drifting pesticides, and negligent chemical discharge during machine washing. OCPs have been proven to be endocrine disruptive substances due to their properties of being carcinogenic and estrogenic in humans (Rajmohan et al., 2020). In addition, OCPs also show toxicity toward aquatic animals and eggshell thinning in birds.

Methoxy-DDT pollution is a major environmental issue because of its persistence, bioaccumulative nature, and possible negative impacts on ecosystems. As an organochlorine pesticide, methoxy-DDT can remain in the environment for long periods, accumulating in soil, water, and sediments. Its persistence can potentially cause long-term contamination, putting diverse creatures along the food chain at risk (Ore et al., 2023). Aquatic ecosystems are especially vulnerable because methoxy-DDT may accumulate in water bodies, hurting fish, invertebrates, and other aquatic animals. Chronic exposure to methoxy-DDT can affect physiological systems, including reproduction and immunological responses, resulting in diminished population viability and biodiversity loss. Furthermore, methoxy-DDT's potential to biomagnify as it travels up the food chain raises the danger of exposure to higher trophic levels, such as in humans, creating physiological problems. For example, neurological problems in children, such as hyperactivity and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), as well as lower birth weight and stunted growth. While direct exposure to methoxy-DDT is uncommon due to its restricted usage, its presence in the environment remains a concern. It needs constant surveillance, research, and regulatory efforts to reduce its impact and maintain ecosystem health and integrity.

Many techniques have been employed to remove organic pollutants from contaminated water and wastewater, including adsorption, coagulation, filtration with coagulation, precipitation, ozonation, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, and advanced oxidation technologies (Ajith and Paulraj, 2022). Since these techniques need significant capital and operating expenses, it has been discovered that their application is limited. Adsorption is one of the most sought-after wastewater decontamination techniques due to its ease, simplicity, and excellent removal efficiency (Dharmapriya et al., 2021). Industrial wastewater is commonly treated with adsorption to eliminate organic and inorganic pollutants. Therefore, designing efficient and cost-effective adsorbents is a top goal for research. Among several adsorbents, including biochar, clay, and zeolites, mesoporous silica (MS) is a material with enormous surface areas, long-range organized pore channels, and tunable pore diameters (Kresge et al., 1992).

Nanoparticles and nano-based formulations have lately been raised as an attractive field of research for removing aqueous pollutants. Nanoparticles have a remarkably more significant surface-area-to-volume fraction and a shorter dispersion path than traditional adsorbents of micrometer size, leading to excellent adsorbent capacity, improved removal efficacies, and dynamics. Carbon dots (CDs) are 0D materials with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and surfaces that are passive to functional moieties containing oxygen, such as carboxylic and hydroxyl groups. CDs have good aqueous stability, are facile to functionalize, less toxic, and show strong photoluminescence, variable color emission, and excellent photostability (Manayil Parambil et al., 2023). CDs offer several benefits over other nanomaterials since they can be readily functionalized and have simple, rapid production processes. Moreover, unlike conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are chemically inert and not photo-bleachable (Manayil Parambil et al., 2023; Siddique et al., 2018). Hence, the objective of the present study is to explore the potential of CDs-embedded mesoporous silica (MS) nanohybrid for removing aqueous methoxy-DDT from real water matrix. Hydrothermally synthesized CDs from fresh Banyan tree leaves were embedded on MS based on surface functionalization properties. From our understanding, it is the first report of a CDs-based organic sphere as a promising adsorbent for aqueous methoxy-DDT adsorption for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs and MS-CDs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized from aqueous leaf extracts of Ficus benghalensis by a microwave-assisted high-temperature hydrothermal process, as previously reported by our laboratory (Ajith et al., 2020). Mesoporous silica-carbon dots (MS-CDs) conjugate material was prepared by treating CDs with mesoporous silica (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Ltd) as the solid substrate. In brief, 7 mL of synthesized CDs (20 mg, w/v) (pH 7) were sonicated to avoid aggregation and then added dropwise to 3g (w/w) of silica and left to react for 2 h at 30 °C with continuous stirring. The reactant mixture was then centrifuged. The MS-CDs hybrid in the pellet was filtered, washed with ultrapure water for three times, and dried at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized CDs and MS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-1800 Shimadzu Corp., Japan) with a wavelength between 250 and 600 nm was used. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (single-beam Cary Eclipse, Agilent Corp., USA) was used to analyze photoluminescence at an excitation wavelength of 317 nm. The hydrodynamic radius (Rh) was analyzed using 90° at 647 nm by photochrom dynamic light scattering equipment. Stokes-Einstein equation was applied to quantify Rh. The Zetasizer device (Malvern, UK) measured zeta potential value between negative and positive 200 mV potential. The functional groups on CDs were investigated with Agilent Cary 630 FTIR in the 400–4000 cm-1 range. JEOL 2100F TEM instrument performing at 200 kV was utilized to capture CD images. MS-CDs hybrid was characterized to understand the conjugation mechanism, and FTIR analysis was performed. To study the effectiveness of MS binding to CDs, the absorption spectra of CDs before conjugation and those of the supernatant produced after centrifugation were noted. Elemental composition was performed via XPS using the XPS Omicron NanoTechnology GmbH, Germany. Thermofisher, Apreo 2S 2100F FESEM instrument performing at 200 kV was utilized to capture images.

Selectivity of the MS-CDs towards endocrine disruptors was analyzed by treating them with bisphenol A, methyl parathion, dichlorvos, and methoxy-DDT (all chemicals were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Ltd). In a 500 mL beaker, the above-mentioned standard solutions of endocrine disruptors (100 mg/L and 7 pH) and 0.5 g of MS-CDs were stirred at 250 rpm using a horizontal shaker (Labline 3527 Orbital Incubator Shaker, USA) overnight. The adsorption efficiency was calculated using a gas chromatograph mass spectrometer (GCMS) (Shimadzu QP-2010 Plus with Thermal Desorption System TD-20). We observed significant adsorption only with methoxy-DDT. Adsorption isotherm and kinetic studies were conducted in batches (using MS-CDs as adsorbent and standard methoxy-DDT solution as adsorbate) to evaluate the adsorption rate and characteristics. 0.5 g of MS-CDs was mixed with 500 mL of a 100 mg/L methoxy–DDT solution in methanol for the adsorption kinetic experiment. The remnant concentration was measured at 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 240, and 300 min reaction periods. 0.5g of MS-CDs adsorbent was treated with 500 mL of 5–100 mg/L methoxy-DDT solution with stirring for the adsorption isotherm experiment. The remnant concentration was measured after 24 h of reaction time. Moreover, pH variation studies were performed with 100 mg/L Methoxy-DDT solutions at 30 °C. By adding 0.5 M HNO3 or 0.5 M NaOH solution, the initial pH (6.8) was adjusted to acidic (2.21) and basic (12.7). Fractions were collected at regular periods (30 min), centrifuged (Eppendorf centrifuge 5418 R, Germany) for 5 min at 5000 rpm, and filtered using a 0.2 μm filter. After filtration of the supernatant, methoxy-DDT concentrations were determined using GC-MS analysis. The fractions are extracted by 15 mL n-hexane and concentrated to 1 mL using a rotatory evaporator. The column, injector, and detector were 250, 280, and 300 °C, respectively. The carrier gas (nitrogen) flow rate was constant at 2.0 mL/min, with a 25 mL/min makeup gas flow rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Column purification of Yamuna River water using MS-CDs adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The Yamuna River water sample was used for actual environmental sample analysis. The Yamuna water sample was taken from Kalindi Kunj in Delhi, India. After several optimization processes, a fixed-bed column previously described by our laboratory was created with certain modifications (Ajith et al., 2020). A 15-cm long glass column with an inner diameter of 2-cm was used to create a Small Bed Adsorber (SBA) column for the scientific investigation. Glass wool was used to support the produced MS-CDs adsorbent in the glass column. Three SBA columns were made, with different quantities of MS-CDs (7 g and 10 g) as the adsorbent to vary column length. After spiking the Yamuna water with 100 mg/L of methoxy-DDT, the solution was passed in the respective columns at room temperature under a constant flow rate (30 mL/min), and fractions (25 mL) were collected for a defined period. Collected fractions were centrifuged for 2 min at 5000 rpm and filtered using a 0.2 μm filter. After filtration of the supernatant, the methoxy-DDT concentrations were determined by GC analysis. Further, the breakthrough curve was plotted, and several parameters were computed to quantify the characteristics needed to construct a fixed-bed adsorption column on a large scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption and reuse of spent MS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As desorbents, 0.5, 1, and 2M HNO3 solutions were prepared, and 5 mg of spent MS-CDs adsorbents were added to 100 mL of desorbent solutions (Cui et al., 2015). At 25 °C, the heterogeneous mixture was agitated at 100 rpm for 6 h. After that, 5 mL of the supernatant was collected at specified intervals, and the concentration of methoxy-DDT in the desorbent was measured to calculate the desorption equilibrium time. The regenerated adsorbent was reused for five cycles in the same column condition to perform the adsorption procedure, and the adsorption efficiency at each cycle was calculated.

CDs are biogenically synthesized from Ficus benghalensis (L.) extract through microwave-mediated hydrothermal synthesis. Microwave use speeds carbonization with less irradiation due to dielectric heating. Leaf extract's composition aids fast dehydration, facilitating rapid carbonization and CDs formation (S et al., 2017; Hoan et al., 2019). After completing the reaction in the 1000 W microwave reactor system for 2 min, the color of the leaf extract changed to brownish-yellow. This is the preliminary observation for synthesizing CDs (Fig. 1 b Inset), which was confirmed by blue-green fluorescence on irritation with UV light (Fig. 2 b). The synthesized nanoparticle is purified through 0.1 μm syringe filtering and dialyzing against water. UV–visible absorption spectra show peaks at 315 nm and 280 nm (Fig. 1a). Peaks at 315 nm are credited to π-π* transition of sp2 hybridized carbon, and 280 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of heteroatom hybridization, such as N and S (Sun et al., 2006). As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the spectrum of CDs' photoluminescence excitation (PLE) is analyzed by studying emission at 315 nm. The PLE spectra of synthesized CDs display an emission peak centered in the 390–510 nm region. According to the literature, when the hydrothermal temperature drops, the wavelength of the emission redshifts (Hoan et al., 2019). In general, temperature affects the particle size of the CDs, and CDs of larger sizes emit longer wavelengths because of the quantum confinement effect.

The minuscule black dots in the TEM image show the CDs synthesized (Fig. 1c and d). There were no discernible lattice structures in the CDs based on HRTEM images, supporting the amorphous nature of CDs. Bright-field pictures of amorphous carbon-based materials are rarely distinguishable because of the poor contrast between CDs and carbon-coated copper grids. Nonetheless, the image's distinct black dots suggest the creation of concentrated bunches of carbon atoms that are homogeneously distributed. Fig. 1d (Inset) shows a diffused halo free of any spots or rings in the selected area electron diffraction (SEAD) pattern, characteristic of an amorphous sample. As seen in the HRTEM image, these seemingly amorphous clusters are essentially spherical, with sizes ranging from 2 to 3 nm. According to previous studies from our laboratory, hydrothermally synthesized CDs showed poor crystallinity (Ajith et al., 2020). Zeta potential was analyzed to be −25 mV, which advocates synthesized CDs' excellent dispersibility. Previous studies show highly dispersive nanoparticles have zeta potential values of −30 mV to +30 mV (Clogston and Patri, 2011). Further, the mean hydrodynamic radius (Rh) analyzed was 2.2 ± 5.4 nm, which agrees with the TEM investigation (Fig. 1d), wherein the average particle size was 2.5 nm. FTIR is performed to analyze the surface functional groups and conjugate CDs with MS (Fig. 2b). The functional groups obtained in the FT-IR spectra of CDs are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs modified mesoporous silica (MS-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: MS-CDs were fabricated by allowing them to interact with one another and relying on the chemical crosslinking phenomena. To eliminate unconjugated CDs particles, the reaction suspension was centrifuged. Due to the adsorption of CDs with MS, the resulting supernatant was pale yellowish, while UV–visible absorption spectra revealed a decrease in absorption intensity CDs, indicating crosslinking (Fig. 2a). MS changed from white to light brown following the interaction (Fig. 2a. Inset). The surface topography before and after the assembling of CDs on MS can be observed in the FESEM images. The FESEM picture of MS shows well-defined mesopores on the surface (Fig. 2b); however, following the assembly of CDs on MS, the surface is modified into a much smoother surface with the mesopores masked (Fig. 2c and 2d.). The average CDs size is 2.5 nm. These particles may readily enter and get entrapped in the pores of MS. We can also observe CDs on the surface rather than in pores. This indicates the successful assembly of CDs on MS.

FTIR study of the conjugates corroborated the result (Fig. 3 a). FTIR spectrum data indicated stable interaction between the MS and CDs complement. A strong peak at 1082 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of MS reflects Si – O – Si asymmetrical stretching and Si–O bands (Zemnukhova et al., 2015), which vanishes following the reaction with CDs. Based on polarizability, we can expect that the surface functional group of CDs will interact with the lone pair electron in the oxygen of siloxane (Si – O – Si) group of MS in the following order: –OH > –NH > –CO > sp2 –CH > CC. The FTIR spectra confirmed that the –OH bending peak in CDs at 1422 cm−1 promotes conjugation with MS. MS has structural rigidity and, in contrast to other absorbents, does not affect the functioning of the conjugating CDs. MS is also resistant to chemical and heat deterioration, allowing it to be utilized in a varied pH without significant changes in characteristics. These benefits facilitated its conjugation to CDs and following methoxy-DDT adsorption.

Fig. 3b and c displays the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and total pore volume distribution of MS and MS-CD, respectively. The MS had a Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (BET) surface area of 203.699 m2/g and pore volume of 0.715 cm³/g; for MS-CDs, the BET surface area is 185.539 m2/g and total pore volume of 0.657 cm³/g. A prominent BET surface area with hexagonal channels was observed in the MS and MS-CDs. The presence of functional groups on the CDs promotes adsorption. This corroborates the FESEM analysis and proposes that the surface functional group is presented on the outer surface and inside mesoporous channels, facilitating successful MS-CDs hybrid formation.

XPS was used to investigate CDs' chemical composition on the MS's surface, as displayed in Fig. 4 . In the survey spectrum, the Si peak arose from the MS substrate, while the C1s and N1s peaks arose from CDs. The presence of N1s is from the protein contents of the precursor, Ficus L. extract. The O1s peak appeared from both MS and CDs. The BEs of C1s and N1s from XPS spectra are 285.5 & 399.0 eV, which agrees with the reported CD value (Tang et al., 2019). The XPS peaks of Si2s, Si2p, O1s, N1s, and C1s core levels were fitted with Gaussian curves. However, shoulder peaks were observed for N1s and C1s. The shoulder peaks of C1s represent C–C and C–O, and the shoulder peaks of N1s represent –NH, ON–C, and C–N–C (Li et al., 2015). These are the possible bonds in and on CDs (Li et al., 2015). Hence, it indicates that no degradation of CDs occurred during the synthesis of MS-CDs.

The overnight treatment of MS-CDs with a standard solution (100 mg/L) of bisphenol A, methyl parathion, dichlorvos, and methoxy-DDT was analyzed for adsorption efficiency. The adsorptive removal efficiency was calculated using Equation (1).
(1)
R e m o v a l
E f f i c e n c y =
C 1
–
C 2
C 1
* 100
where C1 = Initial Concentration; C2 = Concentration after Treatment.

The removal efficiency for bisphenol A, methyl parathion, and dichlorvos were 2.2 %, 3.5 %, and 1.25 %, respectively. A significant removal efficiency of 51.5 % was observed for methoxy-DDT. This displays the selectivity of MS-CDs in the adsorption of the methoxy-DDT compared to other tested endocrine disruptors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial pH on methoxy-DDT adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The initial pH holds a crucial impact on adsorption as it influences the surface charge of the adsorbent in addition to the level of speciation and ionization of the adsorbate(Awual et al., 2014). MS-CDs showed the highest methoxy-DDT absorption capacity of 82 mg/L at pH 2.21, whereas the ability decreases as pH increases (Fig. 5 a).

The surface functionality plays a prominent role in adsorption capability. It is widely known that surface functionalities protonate and deprotonate when the pH of the medium changes (Gupta et al., 2011). The adsorbent surface gets de-protonation increases as the pH of the aqueous medium increases. This causes a high repulsion for methoxy-DDT and, as a result, a reduced absorption capability. Meanwhile, at lower pH values, protonation of the surface of the MS-CDs is improved, resulting in more significant methoxy-DDT adsorption.

Additionally, methoxy-DDT undergoes demethylation in acidic conditions and forms monophenolic and diphenolic derivatives (Fig. 5 b). According to the strength of nucleophilic groups present in MS–CDs' surface, the –NH group interacts first with these methoxy-DDT phenolic derivatives, followed by –OH and –CH. Maximum adsorption was observed at pH 2.21; that particular pH was chosen for further experiments.

Furthermore, the size and shape of the CDs, as well as their distribution within the mesoporous silica matrix, might affect the availability of active sites for methoxy-DDT molecules, facilitating selective adsorption. Furthermore, the hydrophobic property of methoxy-DDT molecules may encourage their preferred sorption onto the hydrophobic surface of MS-CDs over other endocrine disruptors found in aqueous solutions. Overall, the combined impacts of these parameters result in the remarkable selectivity and removal efficiency of MS-CDs nanohybrid material towards methoxy-DDT, as demonstrated by the study's experimental results.

The adsorption of methoxy-DDT on MS-CDs as a factor of reaction time was plotted to estimate the equilibrium reaction time. The kinetics of methoxy-DDT adsorption on MS-CDs were simulated using pseudo-first-order (PFO), pseudo-second-order (PSO), and intra-particle diffusion (IPD) kinetic models (Fig. 6 a, b, and c).

Lagergren proposed the PFO (Lagergren, 1898) rate equation for the adsorption kinetics as follows:
(2)
ln
(
q e
−
q t
)
= ln
q e
−
K 1
t

Ho and McKay's suggested PSO (Ho and Mckay, 1998) rate equation for the adsorption kinetics is:
(3)
q t
=
q e 2
K 2
t
1 +
q e
K 2
t

The IPD model used to evaluate the diffusion process of the pesticides in an aqueous medium proposed by Weber and Morris (WeberJr. and Morris, 1963) is:
(4)
q t
=
K
d i f f
t
1 2
+ C
Where qt = amount adsorbed on adsorbent at the time (t), K1, K2, and Kdiff are PFO, PSO, and IPD rate constant, respectively, and qe = equilibrium sorption uptake.

The determination coefficient (r2) values for the PFO, PSO, and IPD models (Table 2 ) were 0.809, 0.999, and 0.364, signifying that PSO was more suitable than other models. Further, the maximum adsorption capacity obtained from PFO and PSO models was 0.996 mg/g and 65.359 mg/g. The experimental adsorption capacity (64.1 mg/g) did not match the values estimated to replicate the methoxy-DDT adsorption kinetics using the adsorbents MS-CDs from the PFO model. Experimental findings were extremely close to the PSO model's predicted values, and the r2 values were near one. As a result, the PSO model could better describe methoxy-DDT adsorption by MS-CDs than the PFO and IPD models.
The Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin models were used to analyze the findings of the adsorption isotherm experiment for methoxy-DDT on MS-CDs.

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Langmuir, 2002) holds for monolayer adsorption onto a surface with a fixed count of uniform sites, and its linear form is expressed as:
(5)
1
q e
=
1
K L
q max
⋅
1
C e
+
1
q max
Where KL is the Langmuir isotherm coefficient, qe is the quantity adsorbed on adsorbent (mg/g), Ce is the equilibrium concentration of the adsorbate (mg/L), qmax is the Langmuir constant (mg/g) connected to the maximum monolayer adsorption capacity.

Freundlich adsorption isotherm (Kapillarchemie, 1911) is followed by adsorption, where the adsorbate forms a multimolecular layer on the surface of the adsorbent. Its linearized form is expressed as:
(6)
log
q e
= log
k f
+
1 n
log
C e
Where qe is the amount adsorbed on adsorbent (mg/g), KF is the Freundlich isotherm coefficient (mg/g), 1/n is a function of the strength of the adsorption, n is the adsorption intensity, and Ce is the equilibrium concentration of the adsorbate (mg/L). "When n = 1, the partition between the two phases is independent of concentration; when n > 1, it is normal adsorption; and when n = 1, it is cooperative adsorption (Mohan and Karthikeyan, 1997)." "A value of 1< n < 10 indicates that the sorption process is favorable (Goldberg, 2018). "

The Temkin isotherm model (Tempkin and Pyzhev, 1940) suggests that heat reduces linearly with molecules' adsorption as the adsorbent surface is covered more. Therefore, adsorption is characterized by a uniform distribution of binding energies up to extreme binding energy. Its linearized form is expressed as:
(7)
q e
=
R T
b T
ln
A T
+
R T
b T
ln
C e
Where bT is the Temkin constant (J/mol), T is the absolute temperature (K), R is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol K), and AT is the Temkin isotherm constant (L/g).

Fig. 6 (d, e, f) show the adsorption isotherms for methoxy-DDT on the MS-CD adsorbents. Table 2 shows the isotherm parameters qe, KL, n, KF, BT, KT, and r2 as calculated from the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms. The Langmuir model has a more significant correlation coefficient (r2) than the Freundlich and Temkin models, suggesting that the Langmuir model might be applied to a methoxy-DDT solution on a spherical monolayer surface with weak heterogeneity. MS-CDs' maximum adsorption efficiency for methoxy-DDT was 384.6 mg/g at 25 °C and pH 2.21 as perm Langmuir isotherm. The adsorption capacity of different pesticides onto adsorbents can be influenced by several variables, such as the log-octanol-water partition coefficient (log Kow) (Gupta et al., 2011). The log Kow is generally the measure of the materials' water-repelling capacity (hydrophobicity). The higher the log Kow value, as in the case of methoxy-DDT (4.68–5.08) (Nayak et al., 2018), the higher its water-repelling capacity and, subsequently, the lesser solubility of methoxy-DDT. The result is a higher tendency of methoxy-DDT to adsorb onto MS-CDs.

The substantial difference in adsorption capacity between kinetics (pseudo-second order) and isotherms (Langmuir) emerges from their respective emphasis on various parts of the adsorption process. Pseudo-second order kinetics describes the rate of adsorption over time, with a focus on adsorption speed. It relies on a chemisorption mechanism in which adsorption is restricted by surface coverage and controlled by surface energy. The lower adsorption capacity (qe) indicates that the initial adsorption rate may be slower owing to the surface covering constraints or other causes. In contrast, the Langmuir isotherm represents equilibrium adsorption capacity, concentrating on the maximum quantity of solute that may be adsorbed onto the adsorbent surface under ideal conditions. It implies monolayer adsorption on a homogenous surface, with no lateral contact between adsorbate molecules. The increased adsorption capacity (qe) in Langmuir suggests that, under equilibrium circumstances, the adsorbent can accommodate more solute molecules due to favorable surface interactions. As a result, the difference in adsorption capacity indicates the dynamic character of adsorption kinetics rather than the equilibrium state indicated by isotherms, emphasizing the adsorption process's complexity.

Various structural properties of the hybrid material intricately influence the adsorption behavior of methoxy-DDT on MS-CDs. These properties include size, morphology, surface chemistry, and adsorption capacity, all of which play crucial roles in dictating the efficiency and selectivity of the adsorption process. Firstly, the size of the CDs within the MS-CDs hybrid is a significant factor. As mentioned, the synthesis process yields CDs with an average size of 2.5 nm, which provide a large surface area-to-volume ratio, facilitating high surface reactivity and, consequently, enhanced adsorption capacity. Additionally, the small size allows CDs to penetrate the mesoporous structure of silica, thereby accessing a larger adsorption surface area. Surface chemistry, elucidated through FTIR analysis, is pivotal in determining the adsorption behavior. Functional groups on both CDs and mesoporous silica contribute to the chemical interactions with methoxy-DDT molecules. The FTIR spectra indicate the presence of surface functional groups such as –OH, –NH, and –CO, which can participate in hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions with the target pollutant, enhancing adsorption affinity.

Furthermore, the adsorption capacity of the MS-CDs is closely linked to its surface area and pore structure, as revealed by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm analysis. The BET surface area and pore volume measurements indicate a high surface area with hexagonal channels, which is characteristic of mesoporous silica materials. This porous structure provides ample active sites for methoxy-DDT adsorption, while functional groups on the CDs further enhance adsorption capacity by facilitating additional interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Column purification of Yamuna River water using MS-CDs adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of short bed adsorbed columns (SBA), fixed-bed columns with a shorter length, has previously been described (EH and WJ, 1988). SBA aids in studying limiting variables in the column purification process by promoting adsorption in the top area of the column bed (Weber and Smith, 2002). It further aims to enumerate the characteristics needed to construct fixed-bed adsorption columns on an industrial scale (Kaetzl et al., 2020). The results of the studies were evaluated, and breakthrough curves were generated for both 7 g and 10 g bed weights at a 100 mg/L starting concentration. The analysis of the breakthrough curves, which plot the ratio between the remnant concentration of methoxy-DDT and its initial concentration, i.e., (C/C0) as a function of the volume of effluent moved across the column for 7g and 10g bed of MS-CDs, highlights the performance of the adsorption in the small-bed column.

The Yamuna River, characterized by seasonally and spatially variable organic matter content (COD ranging from 2 mg/L to 260 mg/L), from a highly polluted Delhi stretch, is used as a realistic water matrix for evaluating the efficacy of MS-CDs (Yadav and Khandegar, 2018). The instance of the column in which 1500 mL of Yamuna River water spiked with 100 (mg/L) methoxy-DDT solution was passed through 10 g of MS-CDs material is displayed in Fig. 7 b. The mass transfer zone (MTZ) is the MS-CDs' active surface, where methoxy-DDT adsorption occurs. The skipped Yamuna River water flows across a set of new MS-CDs SBA. MS-CDs adsorb methoxy-DDT when they come in contact with the top of the column. The primary sorption zone (PSZ) is the region between the remnant methoxy-DDT concentration at time 1, C1, and the remnant methoxy-DDT concentration at time t, Ct.

As a result, the initial fraction of the sample collected is free of methoxy-DDT, implying that the residual concentration in this area is approaching zero. As the volume of Yamuna River water moving through the column increases, an adsorption zone known as a mass transfer zone (MTZ) forms. The adsorption process is complete in this area, and the concentration of MS-CDs ranges from 100 mg/L to zero, indicating the total exhaustion of the MS-CDs. Depending on the contact period, this adsorption region might span the full height of the column. C is zero because of the remnant concentration at any moment, and the ratio C/Co is zero. Due to the saturation of the material, methoxy-DDT can no longer be adsorbed entirely when the remnant solution flows across the entire layer of the MS-CDs and reaches the bottom. The surface gained conforms to the breakpoint curve, and this point is known as the breakpoint moment. After a period, the column becomes exhausted or depleted, and methoxy-DDT adsorption is no longer possible. The C/C0 ratio is 1 in this case.

The bed capacity (BC) is represented by the part above the breakthrough curve and is expressed by equation (8) .
(8)
BC = G
∫ 0 T
(
C 0
− C
)
ⅆ t
G is the solution rate in L/min, T is the time taken for complete bed saturation, and C0 and C are the inlet and outlet methoxy-DDT concentration in mg/L at time t, respectively.

"The breakthrough curve would be a vertical line (step change) at Ts, which is the time necessary for complete bed saturation under ideal circumstances, assuming an endlessly fast mass transfer rate between solution and adsorbent" (EH and WJ, 1988). In large-scale execution, a bed runs until effluent hits an arbitrarily low concentration value called the breakpoint concentration, the maximum allowed concentration of the effluent. The effluent uptake bed is not entirely utilized at this stage, and the equivalent height of the new bed is referred to as LUB (length of unused bed), represented by equation (9)
.
(9)
LUB =
z
T s
(
T s
−
T b
)

The BC is expressed by
(10)
BC = G
(
C 0
−
C *
)
T s
Where Ts is the time taken for complete bed saturation at infinite quick adsorption, Tb is the breakthrough time, Z is the bed height (7g MS-CDs- 5.25 cm & 10g MS-CDs- 7.5 cm), C* is the concentration of the solution leaving the bed initially.

After analyzing the experimental data, the BC, LUB, Ts, and Tb parameters were computed and reported in Table 3 . We observed that, as the Z (bed height) was increased, the Ts and Tb were also significantly increased. At saturation time, methoxy-DDT adsorbed on the 10 g MS-CDs column was 49.98 mg/g, and on the 7 g MS-CDs column was 41.5 mg/g. This demonstrates that the potential of methoxy-DDT adsorption on the hybrid fabricated increases with an increase in Z.

Various adsorbent materials, both bulk and nanoscale, have been thoroughly investigated for their efficiency in OCP removal, according to reports in the literature. Bulk adsorbents, such as activated carbon, clay minerals, and zeolites, have received much research due to their large surface area, porous structure, and chemical stability, allowing for effective OCP molecule adsorption from aqueous solutions (Bose et al., 2023). Furthermore, nanostructured adsorbents, which include carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide, as well as metal oxide nanoparticles such as titanium dioxide and iron oxide, have higher adsorption capacities due to their smaller particle size and increased surface area-to-volume ratio. CDs and mesoporous silica have emerged as potential OCP removal possibilities among these nanostructures (Bose et al., 2023; Li and Shi, 2024). CDs, made from various carbon sources, including biomass and waste materials, feature unique qualities such as large surface area, variable surface chemistry, and excellent dispersibility, making them efficient adsorbents for OCPs due to strong π-π interactions and surface functional groups. Mesoporous silica, with its well-defined pore structure and vast surface area, provides an ideal environment for OCP molecules to adsorb through physical and chemical interactions, leading to efficient removal. Our study found that combining CDs and mesoporous silica, known as MS-CDs nanohybrids, improves adsorption effectiveness by synergistically utilizing the benefits of both materials, offering a viable method for rehabilitating methoxy-DDT-contaminated water sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption and reuse of spent MS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HNO3 was used as the desorbent, as the literature suggests that a high concentration of H+ can exchange the organic and inorganic adsorbate from the adsorbent (Wang et al., 2015).

We observed with increasing concentrations of HNO3, the desorption efficiency also increased. The desorption rate increased from 77.3 % to 89.56 % by increasing the concentration of HNO3 to 2 M. After desorption and thorough wash with deionized water, the reusability of the adsorbent was checked for five cycles using methoxy-DDT spiked Yamuna water. At the 5th cycle, the removal efficiency was 86.53% (Fig. 7c). This demonstrates that the MS-CDs adsorbent has a cost-effective application value for treating wastewater and may be used repeatedly after regeneration to decrease waste formation.

This work demonstrates a novel, economical approach for the preparation of self-assembled MS-CDs material synthesized using an aqueous extract of F. benghalensis L., as an efficient adsorbent for removing methoxy-DDT from environmental waters. The study demonstrates the superior selectivity and removal efficiency of the MS-CDs hybrid material compared to other endocrine disruptors. Small-bed adsorption (SBA) column experiments confirmed the Langmuir isotherm as the best-fit model. Pseudo-second-order kinetics were observed. Parameters like initial concentration and SBA column height affected adsorption capacity. MS-CDs are found to have high adsorption capacity and reusability. Biogenic CDs have the potential for river water purification, while MS-CDs are cost-effective and promising for industrial-scale pesticide adsorption and can be applied as potential adsorbent for aqueous methoxy-DDT for wastewater treatment. Building on the demonstrated efficacy of MS-CDs in removing methoxy-DDT from aqueous environments, future research can explore its applicability at various scales. Pilot-scale studies could evaluate the effectiveness and regeneration efficiency of MS-CDs in continuous flow systems, mimicking real-world wastewater treatment plants. This would provide valuable insights into the practical applicability and economic feasibility of MS-CDs for methoxy-DDT remediation. Furthermore, the research could be broadened to investigate the removal of other persistent organic pollutants using MS-CDs. The exceptional selectivity exhibited by MS-CDs towards methoxy-DDT suggests its potential for targeted removal of a broader range of contaminants. Exploring its efficacy against other pollutants commonly found in wastewater streams would significantly enhance the environmental impact of this technology by offering a versatile and efficient method for wastewater treatment.

Ajith Manayil Parambil: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Eepsita Priyadarshini: Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Rohit Goutam: Visualization, Resources, Formal analysis. Pei-Chien Tsai: Visualization, Project administration, Conceptualization. Po-Chin Huang: Visualization, Resources, Conceptualization. Paulraj Rajamani: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Yuan-Chung Lin: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Vinoth Kumar Ponnusamy: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: INSPIRE Senior Research Fellowship (Grant No. IF 180104) to AMP from the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Gov. of India, is acknowledged. This work was partially supported by the Research Center for Precision Environmental Medicine, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan, from the Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan and by the Kaohsiung Medical University Research Center Grant (KMU-TC113A01). This research work was also supported financially by the grants NSTC110-2113-M-037-009-, and NSTC 112-2113-M-037-005- from the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan. The authors are thankful to operators and gratefully acknowledge the use of HR-TEM, FE-SEM, pXRD, and XPS equipment provided by the Instrument Center (core facility) of National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan, and instrument facilities provided by National Sun-yet Sen University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan, for this research work. In addition, we also thank KMU-NHRI for their bilateral collaborative research grant support (NHRIKMU-113-I005).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Rajmohan et al., 2020
